params.text:
Complications to watch for

Most people with a PEG have no serious problems, but complications do happen and noticing them early keeps people safe. Around the site, watch for infection (redness, swelling, warmth, pain, or discharge that may smell), leakage of feed or stomach contents around the tube, and granulation — extra red, moist, sometimes bleeding tissue that can grow at the stoma. During feeding, watch for coughing, breathlessness or a wet, gurgly voice, which can point to aspiration.

  • Site infection: redness, swelling, warmth, pain or smelly discharge.
  • Leakage and granulation: feed leaking, or extra red tissue at the site.
  • Aspiration signs: coughing, breathlessness or a wet-sounding voice.

Feeling unwell and dehydration

How the person feels matters just as much as the tube. Nausea, vomiting, bloating or diarrhoea can be caused by the feed running too fast, feed that is too cold, an intolerance, or another illness, and they can make a person miserable and unwell. Watch too for signs of dehydration — a dry mouth, dark or reduced urine, drowsiness, confusion or sunken eyes — which can develop if flushes and fluids are missed or if the person is losing fluid.

You are not expected to work out the cause of these problems, but you are expected to notice them, record what you see, and pass it on. Slowing or stopping a feed that is making someone sick, and reporting it, can prevent a small problem from becoming a serious one. Always describe what you have actually observed — what, when, and how much — so the nurse or dietitian can act.

params.question: Which of these is a sign of a possible PEG site infection that you should report?

answers.text: Redness, swelling, warmth or pain around the stoma, or discharge that may smell.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. These are signs of a possible site infection and should be recorded and reported to the nurse.

answers.text: The person asking what time their next feed is due.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — that is a normal question, not a sign of infection.

answers.text: The external plate sitting in its usual, correct position.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a plate in the correct position is a sign that things are as they should be.

answers.text: Clean, dry, healthy-looking skin around the tube.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — healthy skin is exactly what you are hoping to see, not a sign of infection.
